_provenance.instruction_md_quote: This is a massive project, you should look into its docs and online to understand the context on how this project is being used and why it's trending. Then your next task would be to assess this project for refactoring based on our own philosophy, and to address its shortcomings, like the prompt-injection type attack. Ideally I think this should become a multi-agent workflow where there are many different departments to go through, mirroring human like HR, security and such so that we can mitigate attack risk and break down task in order to let agents do them better
_provenance.instruction_md_paraphrase: Assess this trending project, refactor it to address prompt-injection vulnerabilities, and re-shape it into a multi-agent workflow whose 'departments' (HR-like, security-like, etc.) gate work to mitigate attack risk and decompose tasks for downstream agents.

T2_security_first.verbatim: security first, full architecture can come later
T2_security_first.scope_implication: Security is the load-bearing deliverable in this trial. Full multi-department architecture is descoped to optional/best-effort. Goals that hinge on a complete departmental org chart drop to secondary or anti-gaming-only.

T3_llm_as_reviewer.verbatim: this feels like quite on the defensive. Prompt injection is quite advanced, they could be leading the LLM with multiple back and forth prompts in order to break it. I was thinking of using LLM as security also to review, let'sd discuss
T3_llm_as_reviewer.scope_implication: Pure pattern/regex defense is insufficient on its own. Some LLM-driven review of the agent's tool calls / messages MUST be in the architecture. This becomes a core, load-bearing capability.

T4_testing_query.verbatim: how do you plan to test this with actual LLM? Just answer
T4_testing_query.scope_implication: Diagnostic question only; no new capability obligated. Does not add a goal.

asks_NOT_in_user_messages_so_NOT_graded: No filename, directory, module path, or symbol name was prescribed by the user. The user said 'multi-agent workflow' and 'departments like HR, security' — these are conceptual, not file-system prescriptions. The rubric MUST NOT require src/security/, risk-tiers.ts, reviewer.ts, REVIEWER_SYSTEM_PROMPT, classifyTool, isBashDestructive, checkPatterns, escalateRisk, createSecurityDecisionFlow, or any other oracle-session identifier.
No specific risk-tier vocabulary (low/medium/high) was prescribed — any equivalent gradation (e.g., safe/sensitive/destructive, allow/review/block, tier-0/1/2) satisfies the spirit.

No specific verdict vocabulary (APPROVE/DENY/ESCALATE) was prescribed by the user — that came from the oracle agent's design. Any structured verdict scheme that drives downstream behavior satisfies the spirit.
No specific test framework, file extension (.ts vs .js vs .py), or build system was prescribed.

Human-in-the-loop / human-approval gate was NEVER mentioned by the user — that emerged from the oracle agent's design. v3 does NOT grade on it. (v2 incorrectly included it as a secondary goal traceable only to oracle behavior.)
No specific number of departments / pipeline stages was prescribed.

Pattern-based (regex) detection was NEVER positively requested by the user — the user actually pushed AWAY from it ('feels like quite on the defensive'). v3 does NOT require pattern-based detection as a primary capability; it MAY appear inside the LLM-review path or as a pre-filter, but its absence is not a defect.

_v2_to_v3_diff_summary: v3 strips out v2's lingering oracle-session contamination. Specifically: (a) v2 goal_5 (destructive ops require additional human-approval gate) is REMOVED — the user never asked for human-in-the-loop; that was the oracle agent's design choice. (b) v2 goal_2 (prompt-injection detection as its own first-class goal) is FOLDED into the LLM-review capability rather than being a separate behavioral pillar — the user's T3 message explicitly downgrades standalone pattern matching. (c) Added explicit goal_0 anti-gaming with concrete veto conditions. (d) Added a goal covering the multi-agent / departmental decomposition since the user's instruction.md verbatim asks for it (even though T2 narrows scope to 'security first', the instruction text is the controlling user ask and the secondary multi-agent goal honors it at a lower weight). (e) Weights deliberately do NOT mirror F2P weights in tests/test.sh — that file is a Step-0 reward-shaping artifact pinned to the oracle architecture and is explicitly known to be oracle-specific; v3 is the judge-layer rubric and is sized by user-intent emphasis, not by F2P weight allocation.

_dropped_from_v2.v2_title: Destructive operations require an additional gate beyond LLM review (human approval, dangerous-mode flag, denial-by-default)
_dropped_from_v2.drop_reason: No user message asks for human-in-the-loop. The 'Pattern + LLM + Human' three-layer pattern is an oracle-session-specific design crystallized from the agent's own proposal, not from a user ask. Grading on it would punish any architecture that uses LLM-review or denial-by-default WITHOUT a human-callback hook, which is a legitimate design choice the user neither requested nor prohibited.

_added_for_coverage.title: Decompose the workload across distinct agent roles / departments / stages so that security review is one role among several
_added_for_coverage.add_reason: The instruction.md text literally says 'multi-agent workflow where there are many different departments to go through, mirroring human like HR, security'. v2 dropped this entirely under the T2 'security first' narrowing. v3 retains it as a SECONDARY goal because (i) the instruction.md ask is verbatim, and (ii) T2 only deprioritized — it did not delete — the multi-department vision ('full architecture can come later' explicitly preserves it as deferred-not-cancelled). Weighting at 0.10 reflects this deferred status.

completeness_goals.goal: Anti-effort / anti-workaround guard. The diff MUST be the product of the agent's own deliberate edits during the trial — NOT obtained by checking out an alternate branch, fetching a pre-built security module from a gist or fork, copy-pasting a recognizable upstream/community implementation, or running a single 'curl ... | apply' shell command. Hard vetoes: (a) if trial telemetry shows zero in-session edit/write tool calls AND the final patch is larger than 5 KB of net additions, set judge_score = 0; (b) if any single contiguous block of additions longer than 200 lines exactly matches a public OpenClaw fork or known security-template repository searchable on GitHub, treat as plagiarism and set judge_score = 0; (c) if the diff is dominated (>80% of net adds) by files that look auto-vendored (e.g., a verbatim copy of a third-party security middleware), set judge_score = 0. This goal has zero positive weight — it can only veto, never contribute to score.
completeness_goals.user_provenance: Not user-requested. Standard integrity guard required of every open-ended rubric per project policy. Particularly important here because the open-ended security-design framing makes 'drop in someone else's security module' an attractive shortcut.
completeness_goals.rationale: Open-ended design tasks have no narrow test fixture to game, so the gameable surface is 'paste any well-engineered security module from elsewhere'. Goal_0 closes that surface without constraining legitimate design freedom.

completeness_goals.goal: The diff demonstrably introduces an LLM-driven review step that inspects agent activity (user messages, tool calls, or both) BEFORE the agent's actions are dispatched, and the review step's output materially affects whether or how the action proceeds. The review can be implemented in any shape — a dedicated module, a pipeline stage, a middleware function, a callback wired into the gateway, an agent role inside a multi-agent orchestrator, a class method, a graph node — as long as: (a) there is a clearly LLM-call-shaped code path (sends a prompt to an LLM, expects structured-ish output), and (b) the review output drives a branching decision (allow / block / flag / re-prompt / escalate / annotate). The review does NOT have to use a separate LLM provider; using the same endpoint as the main agent with a security-focused system prompt is a fully acceptable shape (and was the user's explicit preference, see T3 + intent_6).
completeness_goals.user_provenance: instruction.md ("address its shortcomings, like the prompt-injection type attack") + T3 verbatim ("I was thinking of using LLM as security also to review"). This is the single load-bearing capability the user introduced via correction. The user pivoted away from pure pattern-matching specifically because they considered it insufficient against multi-turn injection.
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_inside_goal: Mere presence of the string 'security' in a comment, an unused helper, or a dead-code branch does NOT satisfy. The reviewer code path must be actually invoked from an entry point that processes real agent activity (look for it to be called from a tool-dispatch path, a request handler, a pipeline runner, or have unit tests that exercise it end-to-end). A function definition with zero call sites in the diff is NOT credit. Similarly, a TODO/comment that says 'TODO: add LLM review here' is NOT credit. Dependency-injection of the LLM provider (e.g., passed as an optional constructor parameter, config field, or factory argument) is an acceptable architecture and satisfies the call-site requirement when the integration path is demonstrably wired through real dispatch/handler code (e.g., dispatch → security-guard → default-pipeline → LlmAnalyzerStage). The stage class need not unconditionally invoke the LLM at module load — what matters is that (i) the LLM-call branch inside process() is reachable when the provider is supplied, (ii) the stage is composed into a real runner that is itself called from an entry point, and (iii) either the wiring is exercised by tests (mock provider counts) or the dispatch chain is statically traceable from a top-level handler. A fail-open default (heuristics-only when provider is absent) does not by itself disqualify, provided the LLM path is genuinely wired and reachable.
completeness_goals.rationale: Highest weight because (i) it's the only capability the user introduced via mid-session correction (the strongest signal of priority), (ii) the user's framing of the problem — 'prompt injection is quite advanced, multi-turn manipulation' — is uniquely answerable by LLM review and uniquely NOT answerable by regex/static checks alone, and (iii) the broader 'multi-agent workflow' framing in instruction.md is most cleanly instantiated by this review role.

completeness_goals.goal: Some part of the diff demonstrates that the security/review layer treats different categories of agent activity DIFFERENTLY — i.e., not every action takes the same path through review. Acceptable evidence includes: (a) a risk/severity/sensitivity gradation over tools or actions (any vocabulary — low/med/high, safe/sensitive/destructive, allow/review/block, tier-0/1/2, etc.) that influences routing, (b) a fast-path for clearly-benign reads versus a slower-path for state-mutating operations, (c) a classifier (function, lookup, LLM call, annotation) over actions whose output gates downstream behavior, (d) explicit short-circuit logic for one category that does not apply to another. The shape can be a function, a table, a switch, an annotation on tool descriptors, a class hierarchy — the rubric does not prescribe an API.
completeness_goals.user_provenance: instruction.md ("mitigate attack risk and break down task in order to let agents do them better" — implies differential handling). T3 implication: if every tool call gets the same LLM review, the design is wasteful and degenerate; the user's framing of 'sophisticated multi-turn injection' implies some actions warrant more scrutiny than others.
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_inside_goal: A constant function (returns 'review' for everything) does NOT satisfy — it must demonstrably produce at least two distinct outcomes for at least two distinct categories of input. Likewise, a dead branch labeled 'low risk' that is never reachable from any entry point does NOT satisfy. The judge should be able to point to two concrete code paths and explain how a benign read and a destructive command travel different routes.
completeness_goals.rationale: Second-highest weight because differential routing is implicit in the user's framing but not explicitly demanded with the same emphasis as the LLM-review pivot. It also reflects the 'lean / path of least resistance' philosophy from the skill prompt: not every action needs the heaviest review.

completeness_goals.goal: The diff includes a clear, documented integration point at which a downstream consumer (the existing OpenClaw gateway, request dispatcher, tool-call pipeline, or whatever the codebase's main entry path is) can adopt the security layer. Evidence can be: (a) a wiring change in an existing entry-point file that calls into the new security code, (b) a documented integration guide / README section / inline doc comment that names the exact function or class a maintainer should call and where, (c) a publicly-exported API surface (barrel file, class, builder, middleware factory) that makes the layer integrable without surgical knowledge of internal layout, (d) at least one test that exercises the integration path end-to-end. The diff does NOT have to actually flip a feature flag on; it just has to make integration obvious and small.
completeness_goals.user_provenance: Implicit in instruction.md ("refactoring based on our own philosophy" implies the work integrates into the existing project, not a standalone fork) and explicit in the 'integrity constraints' of the orchestrated-dev skill the user invoked ("Never modify success criteria... Never silently skip requirements" → integration is part of done). Note: the oracle session's gateway-integration step exists for the same reason but the v3 rubric draws its authority from the instruction text, not the oracle session.
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_inside_goal: A file named 'INTEGRATION.md' that consists of generic boilerplate ("follow standard integration practices") without naming concrete entry points / function calls / file locations does NOT satisfy. A barrel file that re-exports symbols nobody can use without reading the source does NOT satisfy. The integration must be operationally usable.
completeness_goals.rationale: Secondary weight: integration matters but it is not the load-bearing technical capability. A perfect LLM-reviewer with bad integration is a more valuable trial outcome than a clean barrel file with no real review behind it.

completeness_goals.goal: The diff exhibits at least the scaffolding of multi-role / multi-stage decomposition that gives the user's 'departments' framing concrete shape. Evidence includes: (a) two or more distinct stages/roles/agents in the security or request-handling path, each with a single clear responsibility (e.g., intake/classification, review, policy enforcement, output filtering, audit logging, etc.), (b) explicit naming/typing/documentation of those stages as conceptually-separate units, (c) an orchestrator/pipeline/runner that composes them. A single monolithic function that does everything in 200 lines does NOT satisfy. Equally, more than 6 stages with no clear reason for the split does not extra-credit — quality of decomposition matters more than count.
completeness_goals.user_provenance: instruction.md verbatim ("multi-agent workflow where there are many different departments to go through, mirroring human like HR, security"). T2 ("security first, full architecture can come later") deprioritizes this — the user explicitly said the full architecture can be deferred — so the goal lives but at lower weight, and is judged on whether the SECURITY portion of the design is itself decomposed into roles, not on whether the whole project was reorganized.
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_inside_goal: Renaming an existing single function to 'HRDepartment' or 'SecurityDepartment' without changing its behavior or splitting it does NOT satisfy. Equally, creating empty stub files named after departments with no implementation does NOT satisfy. The decomposition must be substantive: each role/stage has actual logic, and the roles are composed by some explicit orchestrator/runner.
completeness_goals.rationale: Secondary because T2 deprioritizes it. But it cannot be dropped entirely — the instruction.md text is the canonical user ask and explicitly names this vision. A trial that builds a single security blob with no decomposition is partially honoring the request; one that builds a clean role-decomposition is more fully honoring it.

completeness_goals.goal: The diff is consistent with the 'elegant / clean / lean / path of least resistance' philosophy the user articulated in the skill prompt. Concrete checks the judge can apply: (a) no large dead code blocks, no obviously commented-out alternative implementations left in the diff, (b) no theatrical over-abstraction (e.g., AbstractSecurityReviewerFactoryBuilderProvider) where simpler shapes would do, (c) the diff has at least minimal evidence of testing (unit tests, integration tests, smoke tests, or at least documented manual verification) for the security-critical paths, (d) the additions are scoped to the security/review concern + minimal touch-ups to the existing entry path, not a sprawling rewrite of unrelated subsystems.
completeness_goals.user_provenance: skill prompt (turn 1) which the user invoked deliberately: "All work must be: Elegant — Simple solutions over clever ones; Clean — Readable, well-structured code; Lean — No unnecessary complexity; Path of least resistance — Proven approaches over experiments" and "Never mock/stub production code unless explicitly requested". The user invoking /orchestrated-dev IS the user adopting these constraints.
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_inside_goal: This goal does NOT positively reward beauty for its own sake. It rewards the diff for NOT exhibiting common AI-slop patterns: excessive comments restating obvious code, dead code, mock implementations marked as production, fabricated APIs, over-engineering. The judge should look for at least one of: (i) tests committed alongside production code, (ii) a clear single-responsibility per added file, (iii) reuse of existing project patterns rather than introducing a parallel idiom. Absent ALL of these → low credit on this goal; presence of any → partial; presence of multiple → full.
completeness_goals.rationale: The user explicitly invoked an orchestrated-dev skill whose philosophy IS this. The skill prompt is part of the user-controlled context (the user chose to invoke it), so its quality bar is fair to grade against. Weight kept secondary because it's a cross-cutting hygiene check, not a feature.

_weight_check.weight_distribution_rationale: Single dominant goal at 0.35 (LLM-review) because it was the user's mid-session correction and the strongest priority signal. Second core at 0.25 (differential routing) reflects an implicit but consistent ask. Three secondaries: integration (0.15), multi-agent decomposition (0.10, deprioritized by T2), philosophy/hygiene (0.15). Sum = 1.00 with goal_0 as veto-only.

_weights_not_mirroring_F2P_attestation: v3 weights (0.35 / 0.25 / 0.15 / 0.10 / 0.15) deliberately do NOT mirror the F2P weights in tests/test.sh (0.12 / 0.15 / 0.18 / 0.13 / 0.10 / 0.20 / 0.12). The F2P weights in test.sh are tuned for oracle-architecture file presence and individual primitives (classifyTool, isBashDestructive, etc.); v3 weights are tuned for user-intent emphasis and capability presence. The two weighting schemes intentionally diverge.
_no_oracle_identifiers_attestation: No goal in this rubric mentions: src/security/, risk-tiers.ts, pattern-check.ts, risk-classifier.ts, reviewer.ts, decision-flow.ts, gateway-integration.ts, approval-manager.ts, audit-log.ts, classifyTool, classifyToolRisk, isBashDestructive, checkPatterns, escalateRisk, createSecurityDecisionFlow, REVIEWER_SYSTEM_PROMPT, or any other oracle-session-specific symbol/path. Capability descriptions are deliberately algorithm/API-agnostic.

round_1_findings.concern: Does any goal trace back to a USER message rather than the oracle agent's design?
round_1_findings.verdict: PASS — goal_1 traces to instruction.md + T3; goal_2 traces to instruction.md + T3 implication; goal_3 traces to instruction.md ('refactoring') + orchestrated-dev skill integrity constraints; goal_4 traces to instruction.md verbatim 'multi-agent workflow / departments'; goal_5 traces to the orchestrated-dev skill philosophy the user invoked. Goal_0 is anti-gaming policy, not user-provenance.
PASS — no goal names a file, directory, function, or symbol from the oracle session. Capability language is consistently algorithm/API-agnostic.

round_1_findings.concern: Is the human-approval-gate ask (which was oracle-only, never user-asked) excluded?
round_1_findings.verdict: PASS — v2 goal_5 was explicitly dropped, documented in _dropped_from_v2.

round_1_findings.concern: Does goal_0 actually have anti-gaming teeth or is it vague?
round_1_findings.verdict: PASS — three concrete veto conditions: (a) zero in-session edits + >5KB patch, (b) >200-line verbatim block matching a known fork, (c) >80% of adds in obviously-vendored files.

round_1_findings.concern: Do all positively-weighted goals have an anti-gaming-inside-goal clause?
round_1_findings.verdict: PASS — goals 1-5 each have an explicit anti_gaming_inside_goal field with concrete defeat-conditions (dead code, single-call-site functions, stub files, constant classifiers, boilerplate INTEGRATION.md, renamed-not-restructured monoliths, etc.).

round_1_findings.concern: Do weights mirror tests/test.sh F2P weights, which would be a reverse-engineering smell?
round_1_findings.verdict: PASS — explicitly attested otherwise. v3 weights are intent-emphasis-driven (0.35/0.25/0.15/0.10/0.15) vs F2P's primitive-coverage-driven (0.12/0.15/0.18/0.13/0.10/0.20/0.12).

round_1_findings.concern: Would a trial that built EXACTLY the oracle architecture (src/security/<6 files>.ts with all the named exports) pass v3?
round_1_findings.verdict: PASS — the oracle architecture satisfies all 5 positive goals: LLM reviewer (goal_1), risk-tier routing (goal_2), index.ts + INTEGRATION.md (goal_3), 6-file decomposition (goal_4 weakly, since this is security-only decomposition; the user said 'security first'), and tests + lean code (goal_5). This is the correctness check: a known-good solution must score well.

round_1_findings.concern: Would a trial that built the TRIAL's actual pipeline architecture (src/pipeline/<many stages>) pass v3?
round_1_findings.verdict: PROBABLY PASS — pipeline/stages/llm-analyzer.ts satisfies goal_1; pipeline/stages/security.ts + pipeline/stages/policy.ts together likely satisfy goal_2 (depending on whether they branch on tool category); pipeline/index.ts + a documented integration point satisfies goal_3 (pending judge inspection of doc completeness); the multi-stage layout strongly satisfies goal_4; presence of stage-by-stage tests satisfies goal_5. This is the fairness check: the unfairly-zeroed trial should now score nonzero.

round_1_findings.concern: Is goal_0 too punitive — would it accidentally veto a legitimately-fast agent that wrote concise code?
round_1_findings.verdict: PASS — goal_0 vetoes require BOTH zero in-session edit tool calls AND >5KB net adds (impossible without external code injection), OR a 200-line verbatim block matching a public fork (genuinely-original code wouldn't), OR >80% vendored content. These are all sharp tests, not soft heuristics.

_self_critique_passed.round_1_decision: All checks pass on first review. No rewrite needed.

round_2_findings.concern: Sanity check: re-read every 'tier' assignment against the user provenance — any miscategorizations?
round_2_findings.verdict: PASS — goal_1 (LLM-review) is correctly core because it was the explicit mid-session correction. goal_2 (differential routing) is correctly core because it's implicit-but-strong in 'mitigate attack risk and break down tasks'. goal_3 (integration), goal_4 (multi-agent decomposition), goal_5 (philosophy) are correctly secondary — none are mid-session corrections; all are derivative of instruction.md or the skill prompt.

round_2_findings.concern: Does goal_4 deserve to exist given T2 said 'full architecture can come later'?
round_2_findings.verdict: RETAIN — T2 says 'can come later', not 'is cancelled'. The instruction.md text is the canonical ask and explicitly names the multi-agent vision. Secondary weight (0.10) honors both: the goal exists; it does not dominate.

_self_critique_passed.round_2_decision: All checks pass on second review. Final rubric committed.

_self_critique_log.patch_kind: clarification (no semantic widening of goal body)
_self_critique_log.trigger: Sonnet final-review reviewer flagged a subtle hidden bias: goal_1's anti-gaming clause ('reviewer code path must be actually invoked from an entry point') could be read literally as disqualifying dependency-injection patterns where the LLM provider is an optional constructor parameter. The trial under review uses exactly that DI pattern in LlmAnalyzerStage (optional llmProvider constructor arg). The trial's integration is genuine — dispatch.ts -> runSecurityGuard -> default-pipeline -> LlmAnalyzerStage.process — but a strict literalist judge could mis-score this as 'zero call sites when deployed without provider'. The reviewer rated this MINOR because the goal BODY (clauses (a) and (b)) already accepts the DI shape; only the anti-gaming clause was ambiguous.
_self_critique_log.change_summary: Appended a clarifying sentence (not a goal rewrite) to goal_1.anti_gaming_inside_goal stating that DI of the LLM provider is acceptable when (i) the LLM-call branch in process() is reachable when the provider is supplied, (ii) the stage is composed into a runner that is itself called from an entry point, and (iii) tests with a mock provider satisfy the call-site requirement. Explicitly notes the dispatch -> security-guard -> default-pipeline -> LlmAnalyzerStage chain as a satisfying integration shape. Also noted that fail-open-to-heuristics when provider is absent does not by itself disqualify.
_self_critique_log.non_change_summary: No weights changed. No tiers changed. No goals added or removed. No oracle-session identifiers introduced (the dispatch/security-guard/default-pipeline/LlmAnalyzerStage names are illustrative architecture vocabulary, NOT oracle-session symbols — the oracle session used src/security/<files>.ts with createSecurityDecisionFlow, a wholly different architecture). The clarification narrows the failure modes of a strict judge without widening the credit surface: dead code, zero-call-site definitions, TODO comments, and uninjected stage classes still do NOT satisfy.
verification_self_check: Re-read instruction.md + T3 verbatim: still squarely captured. DI clarification does not soften the user's 'LLM as security reviewer' ask.

Re-read _no_oracle_identifiers_attestation: the patched clause uses 'dispatch / security-guard / default-pipeline / LlmAnalyzerStage' as illustrative shape vocabulary. None of these are oracle-session symbols (oracle used classifyTool, isBashDestructive, REVIEWER_SYSTEM_PROMPT, createSecurityDecisionFlow). The illustrative names happen to align with the trial's architecture, which is fine — the rubric is allowed to describe acceptable architectures including the one this trial built, since the description is capability-shaped, not file-pinned.
Re-read goal_0 anti-gaming: untouched and still load-bearing. The DI clarification is a refinement on goal_1 only.
Re-read _weights_not_mirroring_F2P_attestation: weights unchanged at 0.35/0.25/0.15/0.10/0.15. Sum still 1.0.

Verified the patch does not retroactively credit dead code: the new clause requires (i) reachable LLM branch when provider supplied, (ii) composition into a runner called from an entry point, (iii) test or static traceability. A stage class defined but never composed into a runner still fails.

decomposition_notes: 5 positive goals (1 dominant core at 0.35, 1 core at 0.25, 3 secondaries summing to 0.40) + 1 anti-gaming veto guard at weight 0. Sum of positive weights = 1.00. Every goal carries a user_provenance field tying it to a specific USER message or to the orchestrated-dev skill the user explicitly invoked — no goal is sourced from the oracle agent's choices. Every positive goal has an anti_gaming_inside_goal clause naming concrete defeat-conditions a judge can check by inspecting the diff. The rubric is capability-based (LLM-review present, differential routing present, integration present, decomposition present, hygiene present) and intentionally indifferent to file paths, function names, API shapes, vocabulary choices, and module organization. This is a deliberate departure from v1 (filename-pinned, oracle-mirror) and a refinement of v2 (dropped the human-approval-gate goal that was oracle-only, sharpened anti-gaming, made provenance per-goal explicit, made weight-non-mirroring an explicit attestation).
